---
title: "Endocrine System & Glucocorticoid Receptor Signalling"
subtitle: "Unit 5, Part 1 — BIOL09044 Inflammation & Cancer"
author: "Dr Mark Paul-Clark"
institute: "Nottingham Trent University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../styles-slides.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    hash: true
    transition: fade
    preview-links: auto
    #chalkboard: true
    embed-resources: true
    logo: ""
    footer: "Unit 5: Cortisol | NTU"
---

## Unit Overview {.smaller}

- **Endocrine system revision**
  - Cell signalling revision
- **Molecular actions of steroids**
  - GR receptor complex
- Physiological actions of cortisol
  - Cortisol pathophysiology
- Glucocorticoid drug discovery
  - Drug discovery process

::: {.notes}
This first section covers the foundational endocrine and receptor biology needed to understand cortisol's actions.
:::

---

## Peptide vs Steroid Hormone Signalling

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Peptide Hormones
- Hydrophilic — cannot cross membranes
- Bind **cell surface receptors**
- Rapid actions (seconds–minutes)
- Signal via second messengers:
  - cAMP pathway
  - IP₃/DAG pathway
  - Tyrosine kinase cascades
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Steroid Hormones
- Lipophilic — cross membranes freely
- Bind **intracellular receptors**
- Slower actions (hours–days)
- Genomic regulation:
  - Gene transactivation
  - Gene transrepression
:::

::::

::: {.callout-note}
## Key Distinction
Steroids act as transcription factors — their effects are primarily genomic, altering protein synthesis rather than activating pre-existing signalling cascades.
:::

---

## The Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal (HPA) Axis

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 8
flowchart TD
    A[Hypothalamus] -->|CRH| B[Anterior Pituitary]
    B -->|ACTH| C[Adrenal Cortex<br/>Zona Fasciculata]
    C -->|Cortisol| D[Target Tissues]
    D -.->|Negative Feedback| A
    D -.->|Negative Feedback| B
    
    style A fill:#4a1486,color:#fff
    style B fill:#6b3fa0,color:#fff
    style C fill:#c4a000,color:#000
    style D fill:#2d6a4f,color:#fff
```

- **CRH**: Corticotropin-releasing hormone
- **ACTH**: Adrenocorticotrophic hormone (derived from POMC)
- Cortisol provides **negative feedback** at both hypothalamic and pituitary levels

---

## Cortisol Synthesis & Transport

### Synthesis
- Synthesised from **cholesterol** in the adrenal cortex zona fasciculata
- Follows the steroidogenic pathway via pregnenolone intermediates

### Circulating Cortisol
| Fraction | Percentage | Binding Partner |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| CBG-bound | ~80% | Cortisol-binding globulin |
| Albumin-bound | ~15% | Serum albumin |
| **Free (active)** | **~5%** | Unbound |

::: {.callout-important}
Only the **free fraction** of cortisol is biologically active and able to cross cell membranes to bind the glucocorticoid receptor.
:::

---

## The Glucocorticoid Receptor (GR) Complex

### Inactive State
The GR exists in a **multiprotein chaperone complex**:

- **HSP90** (×2) — maintains receptor conformation and ligand-binding competence
- **HSP70** — assists in receptor folding
- **Immunophilins** (FKBP51/52) — regulate receptor maturation
- **p23** — stabilises the HSP90–GR interaction

### Upon Ligand Binding
1. Cortisol binds the ligand-binding domain
2. **HSP90 and HSP70 dissociate**
3. GR undergoes conformational change
4. Receptor **dimerises**
5. Nuclear localisation signal exposed → **nuclear translocation**

---

## GR Mechanisms of Action

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Transactivation (GRE binding)
GR dimers bind **glucocorticoid response elements** to upregulate:

- Annexin-1 (lipocortin-1)
- IκBα (NF-κB inhibitor)
- MAPK phosphatase-1
- IL-10
- SLPI

→ **Anti-inflammatory gene expression**
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Transrepression (protein–protein)
GR monomers interact with transcription factors to suppress:

- **NF-κB** → reduces TNFα, IL-1β, IL-6, COX-2, iNOS
- **AP-1** → reduces MMPs, collagenase

→ **Suppression of pro-inflammatory mediators**
:::

::::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Clinical Relevance
The therapeutic anti-inflammatory effects of glucocorticoids are primarily attributed to **transrepression**, while many side effects arise from **transactivation**. This distinction drives the search for dissociated GR ligands.
:::

---

## Summary: Part 1

:::{.incremental}
- Cortisol is regulated via the **HPA axis** with negative feedback
- Only **free cortisol** (~5%) is biologically active
- The GR exists in an **HSP90/70 chaperone complex** in the cytoplasm
- Ligand binding triggers **HSP dissociation → dimerisation → nuclear translocation**
- GR acts via **transactivation** (anti-inflammatory genes) and **transrepression** (pro-inflammatory transcription factors)
- The transactivation/transrepression distinction is key to understanding both therapeutic effects and side effects
:::

---

## Self-Assessment

<details>
<summary>**Q1:** Why can steroid hormones cross the cell membrane but peptide hormones cannot?</summary>
Steroid hormones are **lipophilic** (derived from cholesterol), so they dissolve in and pass through the phospholipid bilayer. Peptide hormones are hydrophilic and require membrane-bound receptors.
</details>

<details>
<summary>**Q2:** What would happen to cortisol levels if a patient had an ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma?</summary>
Cortisol levels would be **chronically elevated** because the adenoma produces ACTH autonomously, bypassing normal negative feedback regulation. This is one cause of Cushing's disease.
</details>

<details>
<summary>**Q3:** Explain why the dissociation of HSP90 from the GR is essential for glucocorticoid signalling.</summary>
HSP90 masks the nuclear localisation signal and prevents dimerisation. Its dissociation upon ligand binding allows the GR to **dimerise, translocate to the nucleus, and bind GREs** to regulate gene transcription.
</details>
